speaker: interviewee
text: --saw in the Jewish newspapers, they need a woodturner in Seaford. It's a long way from where I used to live, you know, in the Bronx. You got to take trains to go there, Seaford, Long Island. But I said, let me try it out, see what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took the train. I went to Seaford, a small little town in Long Island. I walked into the shop. Mr. Morris came out, WM Morris. And he had like five, six workers working, all gentile people. And he says, you're a woodturner? And he looked at me again. Because people don't believe when you're young, 20-some years, a boy, you know, a woodturner. You got to have experience many years in woodturning.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, here, go to the lathe. And he had that time a order from a furniture company to make bed posts in cherry wood with the fancy ball on the top for beds, you know. The legs, the headboard, and the footboard, all in four-by-four beautiful turnings in cherry wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I turned it out. And he looked it. He says, I was looking for a man like you for a long time. You got the job. How much a week? He asked me. He asked me, how much shall I pay you? I says, I don't know. You're the boss. He says, I'll start with $90. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave me all the privileges in the shop. The boss himself I didn't like, but to say, [INAUDIBLE] like that. When I used to go home, he used to run with a brush and clean up the wood shavings from my thing. And the other workers used to be a little angry. They didn't like that. They were cabinetmakers, and I was the only woodturner. So they didn't like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to mumble. And they were a little, you know, not Jew-happy. Because I heard a couple of times in this shop, and they are very-- he's too good to that Jew boy, they said. But not-- you know, not fighting with me or anything. So I worked there, like, seven, eight months. Any time a relative came to see me in Seaford, he said, Morris, take off half a day with pay. Morris, take off a whole day. You have a relative. He was very good to me. So then I got a letter from Florida, from a great uncle, from my mother's uncle. He lives here in Florida in the southwest. Morris, you're not-- you left from this big family, so little people. I would like you to come to Florida. What are you staying? It was January at that time. In New York, it's cold, freezing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Come. In Florida, we'll find you work here. Don't worry, you have a trade. We'll find you work. Come to Florida. Take a vacation and you'll see. Excuse me. So in that shop was working part-time, a pilot or a pilot attendant, a second pilot, who works for the airline, for National. He says, Morris, you have invitations to Florida, then you got to go to Florida. I'll get you a, a ticket from the National Airline. I'll take you to the airport, and I'll transport you to your uncle to see your relative.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Bill. And he took me to the airport all bundled up in winter clothes. Came to Miami, the airplane. I see people walking around in shorts, with the palm trees, with oranges, with bananas growing. I said, what is it, a paradise country? I've never seen it in wintertime in my life, oranges growing, with all kind of things. And my Uncle Frank with short pants, you know, with the open here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his son came in a taxi. They took me-- they took me home. And he says, Morris, we are going to find you a job in Miami. You're not going back in the cold, freezing place. I says, I cannot. I must go back and tell my boss. He's a very nice gentleman and very nice person. Give him at least notice two weeks, at least that I'm quitting. He says, OK, Mo, don't make it seem as-- you're in America. Don't make it seem as-- don't be a gentleman too much. I cannot do that. I went back. I says, Mr. Morris, I have a uncle in Florida, and he wants me to stay. He's a older man. You know, old people, you know, they want to be around young people. Made up, you know, a little [INAUDIBLE], a little truth, too. And he wants me to be near his side. I want to leave for Florida. He says, OK, Morris. I'm glad you gave me two weeks. I'm going to advertise in the newspapers. After the two weeks, I took my couple little things I had there and came to Florida. Came to Florida. Florida was a place only for tourists, not for woodturners. Everybody promised me, they have so much. Every little cabinet shop who had a-- to fix a leg once in a year says, ooh, we have so much work. We don't know what to do. We send it-- we got to send it to New York and wait three weeks for that piece of wood. Everybody looks out for themself. If he gets a [INAUDIBLE] woodturner here in Miami, he wouldn't have to send it to New York and wait two weeks. So the five or 10 cabinetmakers promised me there was so much work. There was almost no work at all. But I took a job. And little by little, I start to go around shops, you know, to show my work. Found a couple of other cabinet shops. But it was still not enough to make a living. Then I-- I was-- I met my wife on the beach. And we got married. And I had a child. And I need money. And couldn't make a living here in Miami. All tourists. It's not like now. It's got industry and Hialeah factories with all the things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, a lady came in-- in the shop, which I made a little shop I had in Florida. Made in a small, little place. A lady came in. She said, can you turn me out lawn mower rollers for my old lawn mower? The old lawn mower used to have wooden rollers underneath. Now they don't have that. The old lawn mowers used to have-- each lawn mower had four or five wooden rollers. With the rollers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know. It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to show you there, you see. The rollers-- the lawn mower repair people needed-- they used to get them from New York and pay $0.50 apiece.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'll make it-- the-- for the lady. I made her the five rollers. And I charged her a couple of dollars. And I used good wood, you know, maple out to make the rollers. And she says, that's the best rollers I had in my life. So I said, she's so impressed with the rollers I turned out for the lawn mower. Let me find a place does have lawn mowers and shops who fix the lawn mowers. I'll go to them and I'll tell them I make rollers. So they gave me all of them. I brought a sample. And they all gave me-- make me five. Make me three. Make me eight. Make me 10. But then I start to figure out, that piece of wood alone cost $0.20. So it's only left $0.30 to turn a roller. It's not enough. What I do? To raise them? I cannot. They get them from New York, even my rollers are better, nicer. So one day, it came to my mind, I remembered that my uncle in New York told me a story that in the wartime, what it make him-- what it made him stand on his feet to make a dollar is he made balls for the army. It's called Skee balls. The soldiers on the ships played Skee balls. They roll a ball and it goes in in a-- in numbers. And what did he use for the balls to make? The old bowling pins from the bowling alleys, what they got to throw out, the little cheap. And the bowling pins got to be in good shape when you play bowling. So he went to the old bowling alleys in New York and he got them for nothing, because otherwise, they were going to throw it out. And he made the bowling-- the balls for the Skee balls. So it came to my mind in Miami, let me go and find where the bowling alleys. I went on 36th Street. I walked in a bowling alley. The man says-- I said to the man, do you have old bowling pins you want to get rid of that? He says, yeah, about-- we were going about to throw them out, because the fire department is on my neck already all week. It's a fire hazard. Take all of them. So I took the bowling pins. And I had a old jalopy station wagon, a broken one. I loaded up it was-- [INAUDIBLE] all the pins. I brought the pins to my shop, to the little shop I had. I cut away the neck from the pin. And the heavy ball-- the heavy belly on the pin, I turned out the lawn mower roller, the wooden roller. So it didn't cost me no money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I start to supply all the lawn mower repair people. And then I found a store where they sell lawn mower supply for the lawn mower shops. And I came in. I said to the man-- his name was Walter, a nice person. And they had [INAUDIBLE]. And they saw my roller. After I turned them, I took machine oil from the cars, you know, what you use the oil, and I dipped in machine oil, and I wiped them out, and they came out like a piece of amber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I brought them. He says, wow. How much you going to charge it? I said, $0.50. How many you got? I says, just a sample. He says, make me 50 right away. 50 pins is $50. It's $25, $0.50 apiece.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made it. I brought it. He says, make me a hundred. And that man-- I was about to leave already Miami, because I couldn't make a living. That man, Walter, I'll never forget him all my life. I'm looking for him. I cannot find him. I'd like to give him a party. That man put me on my feet to stay in Miami and to stay and make a living and to be able to go in the grocery. Before I used to go in the grocer and I bought six items, and two I had to put back on the shelf. I didn't have money to pay. And when that man start to give me to make the lawn mower rollers, a new life opened up for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let me ask you some questions. Uh, your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helene.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] and [? Farb. ?] Helene [? Farb. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this time in your life, what was your-- what name were you using in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rutschaisky. No, Rich, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it become Rich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rich, when I changed my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time was 1949-- in 1950-- 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a child, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Howard. He's running my shop now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Howard born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Howard was born in 1955 in February the 2nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married. And he's got three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are his children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's divorced, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are his children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name, Donna, Shawn, and Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren? Other children? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the [? Helene. ?] She's got the dancing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she's married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Amer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And does she have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have-- do you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a son. He works in Bell Lab.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Raymond.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married. And he's got two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brian and Garry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a younger daughter, Holly. She lives in Miami. And she's got two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is Kaitlyn and Hannah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything-- any message you would like to leave with, with your children or with the world today?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the world today and a message I would like to leave. If you're honest [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and humble and you treat people the right way, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you're about to be successful. And don't lie. You promise, you do the job, and make people happy. If the people are not happy, fix it, make them happy. And you got work, and you got respect.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is my key to my success, which is not such a tremendous success, you understand? But I'm very happy what I have comparing, you know, my life, you understand? A man who came-- I didn't have money for a stamp. And I have, you know, property, and a beautiful home, and a factory, and beautiful children, and grandchildren. And all those things is only by being fair.

speaker: interviewee
text: And America. There's no other place where a person can have a chance [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to achieve what he's planning to achieve. There's no other country. America is the only one. And that's why I say all the time, when I have a good day, only in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only thing what I say all the time, my personal life is like a little dog. Do you-- or a big dog. Do you see sometime a dog, he wants to get cross over the street? Big traffic. And he looks from all sides. And he's so careful. And the car hits him and he gets killed. And sometime a little dog, another dog, is about to be careful. He wiggles himself through between all the cars. One car stopped or made-- almost made a accident. Another car almost made a accident. A third one's screaming. They're hollering, look at that lousy little dog. You almost made me killed. And the little dog walks himself through on the other side of the sidewalk. And he walks away, not a touch. And he comes through alive. And that's all the time in my mind, because I've seen, with my own eyes, fate of a little dog. And that was like my fate. I went through all kind of things and I wiggled myself through, with God's help or luck or something is there. And I'm here. And I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
